ja-soft-rejection.observation: Prospect signals indirect deferral — likely nemawashi in progress
ja-soft-rejection.culturalFramework: Japanese business culture: indirect communication. Phrases like 'that's interesting' or 'many considerations' often mask disagreement or lack of readiness. Direct confrontation is avoided to preserve harmony (wa).
ja-soft-rejection.tacticResponse: Stop pushing. Pivot to sending written materials. Suggest: 'I'll send a summary document. You can review with your team. When would a follow-up call work?'

ja-silence-processing.trigger: 5+ seconds of silence after you speak
ja-silence-processing.culturalFramework: High-context communication in Japanese business. Silence is valued for thoughtful consideration. Rushing to fill silence is seen as disrespectful.
ja-silence-processing.tacticResponse: Do NOT fill the silence. Wait 3-4 more seconds. Then ask open-ended: 'What are your thoughts?' or 'Please share your feedback.'

trigger: thank you for taking the time
ja-formal-address.observation: You're demonstrating respect for hierarchy and structure
ja-formal-address.culturalFramework: Japanese culture values formal address and respect for position. Using proper titles (san, sama) and formal language builds trust and credibility.
ja-formal-address.tacticResponse: Continue this approach. Maintain formal tone throughout. Avoid casual Western familiarity.

trigger: we prefer to decide as a team
need to discuss this with our team
ja-group-consensus.culturalFramework: Japanese business: decisions require consensus (nemawashi). Individual agreement is insufficient. Multiple stakeholders must approve via the ringi process.
ja-group-consensus.tacticResponse: Ask: 'Who else needs to be involved in this decision?' Offer to present to the full committee. Allow extra time for internal alignment.

ja-written-confirmation.observation: Prospect prefers documentation over verbal commitment
ja-written-confirmation.culturalFramework: Japanese business culture values written documentation as formal commitment. Verbal agreements are preliminary; writing is binding and trustworthy.
ja-written-confirmation.tacticResponse: Prepare detailed written proposal immediately. Include all terms, timeline, and next steps. Follow up in writing after verbal discussion.

ja-timing-respect.observation: Pacing signal — respect the deferred timeline
ja-timing-respect.culturalFramework: Japanese business respects timing and patience. Pushing immediately is seen as aggressive. Acceptance of deferred timelines shows respect.
ja-timing-respect.tacticResponse: Honor the timeline. Send a polite reminder email on the agreed date. Do not follow up sooner; it signals disrespect.

ja-personal-relationship.observation: Prospect values personal connection before business
ja-personal-relationship.culturalFramework: High-context culture: relationships precede transactions. Trust must be built through face-to-face interaction and consistent follow-up.
ja-personal-relationship.tacticResponse: Offer in-person meeting. Find common ground personally. Be patient with the relationship-building phase.

ja-price-sensitivity.observation: Prospect is price-conscious but not necessarily rejecting
ja-price-sensitivity.culturalFramework: Japanese buyers negotiate carefully to maximize value. Price discussion is normal; they expect room for negotiation and added value.
ja-price-sensitivity.tacticResponse: Acknowledge cost sensitivity. Offer added value (training, support, customization) rather than discounts. Show ROI carefully with data.
